abstract: To address the issues of hallucination and factual inaccuracy in large language models (LMs), we propose a novel framework for **Forward-Looking Active REtrieval augmented generation (FLARE)**. While standard retrieval-augmented LMs typically retrieve information only once based on the initial user prompt, this "retrieve-and-generate" bottleneck is often insufficient for long-form generation where information needs evolve as the text progresses. FLARE overcomes this by actively deciding when and what to retrieve throughout the generation process. Specifically, the system monitors the confidence of the LM; if the model generates a temporary sentence containing low-probability tokens, FLARE treats that sentence as a placeholder for future intent and uses it as a query to retrieve relevant external documents. The model then regenerates the sentence based on the fetched knowledge. We evaluate FLARE across four complex, long-form knowledge-intensive tasks, including multi-hop reasoning, open-domain summarization, and long-form question answering. Our results demonstrate that FLARE consistently outperforms or remains competitive with existing single-time and multi-time retrieval baselines, highlighting its effectiveness in maintaining factual atttribution in extended text generation.
